text: CRAVENVILLE, a village in the above co., 136 m. SE by E of Raleigh.
CRAWANG, a village of the island of Java, 35 m. SE of Batavia.
CRAWCROOK, a township in the p. of Ryton, in the co. of Durham, 3\frac{1}{2} m. SSE of Yarm. Area 2,460 acres. Pop. 290.

CRAWFORD, or Crawford-Douglas, a parish forming the S extremity of Lanarkshire. Area 76,000 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,671; in 1841, 1,684, of whom 950 were in the v. of Leadhills. The Lowthers [alt. 2,500 ft.] are chiefly in this p., of which the greater part consists of hills and moors. At Leadhills are the most extensive lead-mines in Scotland.

CRAWFORD, a county in the NW part of the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., intersected by French creek and its branches. Area 1,016 sq. m. Its cap. is Meadville. Pop. 31,724.—Also a co. in the W part of the centre of Georgia, skirted on the SW by Flint river. Area 360 sq. m. Pop. 7,981.—Also a co. toward the N part of the state of Ohio, intersected by the Sandusky river. Area 590 sq. m. Pop. 13,152. The cap. is Bucyrus.—Also a co. in the S part of Ohio, skirted by the Ohio river on the SE. Area 432 sq. m. Pop. 5,282. The cap. is Fredonia.— Also a co. in the SE part of Illinois, skirted by the Wabash on the E. Area 426 sq. m. Pop. 4,422.— Also a co. in Wisconsin territory, skirted by the Mississippi on the W, and the Wisconsin on the S. Pop. 1,502. Its cap. is Prairie-du-Chien.—Also a co. in the SE of Missouri, watered by the Maramec. Area 1,650 sq. m. Pop. 3,561. The cap. is Steelville. Iron ore abounds in this district.—Also a co. in the NW of Arkansas, intersected by the Arkansas river. CRE 726 Area 7,800 sq. m. Pop. 4,266. The cap. is Van- Buren.—Also a township in Orange co., in the state of New York, 92 m. S by W of Albany. Pop. 2,075. -Also a township in Washington co., in Maine. Pop. 500.—Also a township in Coshocton co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,134.—Also a v. in Crawford co., in Ohio. Pop. 812.—Also a township in Buchanan co., in Missouri. Pop. 930.

CRAWFORD (FORT), a military port of the United States on the Prairie-du-Chien, in Wisconsin territory, in N lat. 43° 3′ 31", W long. 90° 52′ 30", at an alt. of 580 ft. above sea-level. The mean annual temp. of this place, on an average of 11 years' observations, is 47° 35'; that of summer 72° 38'; of winter 20° 69'.

CRAWFORD-JOHN, a parish in the upper ward of Lanarkshire. Area 21,123 Scots acres. Pop. in 1801, 712; in 1841, 993, of whom 135 were in the v. of Abington, and 137 in the v. of C. Cairntable, in the NW corner of this p., has an alt. of 1,650 ft. above sea-level.
CRAWFORD'S BURN, a village in the p. of Bangor, co. Devon, 2 m. W of Bangor. Pop. 188.
CRAWFORD'S MILLS, a village in Westmoreland co., in Pennsylvania, U. S., 193 m. W of Harisburg.
CRAWFORD-TARRANT, a parish in Dorsetshire, 31 m. SSE of Blandford-Forum. Area 600 acres. Pop. 67.

CRAWFORDSVILLE, a town in Talliafero co., in the state of Georgia, U. S., 62 m. N by E of Milledgeville.—Also a town in Montgomery co., in the state of Iowa, 45 m. NW by W of Indianapolis. Pop. 1,327. It is the seat of Wabash college, founded in 1833.
CRAWICK, a stream in Dumfries-shire, rising on the borders of Lanarkshire, and flowing SW to the Nith, which it joins at Sanquhar, after a course of 10 m.
CRAWICK MILL, a village in the p. of Sanquhar, in Dumfries-shire, a 1 m. NW of Sanquhar. Pop. 144.
CRAWINKEL, a town of Coburg-Gotha, 5 m. W of Plauen. Pop. 1,059.

CRAWLEY, a township in the p. of Eglingham, in Northumberland, 8 m. W by N of Alnwick.—Also a hamlet in the p. and 13 m. WNW of Witney, in Oxfordshire. Pop. 252.—Also a p. in the co. of Southampton, 5 m. NNW of Winchester. Area 4,170 acres. Pop. 483.—Also a p. in Sussex, 8 m. NNE of Cuckfield. Area 820 acres. Pop. 449.
CRAWLEY-HUSBORN, a parish in Bedfordshire, 2 m. NNE of Woburn. Area 1,520 acres. Pop. 656.
CRAWTON, a village in the p. of Dunottar, in Kincardineshire. Pop. 77.
CRAY, a hamlet in the p. of Devynnock, in Breconshire, 9 m. W of Brecknock. Pop. 502.—Also a stream in the co. of Kent, which joins the Darent 2 m. NNW of Dartford.
CRAY (NORTH), a parish in Kent, 1 m. NE of Cray-Foots. Area 1,160 acres. Pop. 358.
CRAY (Sr. Mary's), a parish in Kent, 2 m. S of Cray-Foots. Area 2,220 acres. Pop. 517.
CRAY (Sr. Paul's), a parish in Kent, 5 m. E of Bromley. Area 630 acres. Pop. 564.
CRAY-FOOTS, a parish in Kent, 111 m. S of London. Area 620 acres. Pop. 358.

CRAYFORD, a parish and village in Kent, 13 m. E by S of London. Area of p. 2,380 acres. Pop. 2,408.—The v., situated on the Cray stream, has extensive bleaching-grounds, and large calico-printing establishments.
CREACOMBE, a parish in Devon, 8 m. SE of South-Molton. Area 3,190 acres. Pop. 58.
CREAGH, a parish in co. Cork, adjoining the town of Skibbereen. Area 5,802 acres. Pop. 3,567. -Also a p. in co. Roscommon, adjoining the town of Ballinasloe. Area 8,867 acres. Pop. 2,888.
CREAGH-DOSS, a village in the parish of Taughboyne, in co. Donegal. Pop. 128.
CREAKE (NORTH), a parish in Norfolk, 3 m. SSE of Burnham-Westgate. Area 3,460 acres. Pop. 648.
CREAKE (South), a parish in Norfolk, 3 m. SSW of Burnham - Westgate. Area 3,820 acres. Pop. 940.
CRE'ANCES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Lessay, 10 m. NNW of Contances. Pop. 2,343.
CRE'ANTON, a river of France, in the dep. of Yonne, rising near Sormery, and flowing SSW to the Armançon, which it joins on the r. bank after a course of 12 m.
CREATON, a parish in Northamptonshire, 8 m. NNW of Northampton. Area 790 acres. Pop. 505.
CREAZZO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, deleg. and 2 m. WNW of Vicenza. Pop. 1,110.
CRECHE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of La-Chapelle-de-Guinchay, 4 m. SSW of Macon. Pop. 1,008.
CRE'CHY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Varennes. Pop. 449. Coal is wrought here.

CRE'CY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Somme, arrond. of Abbeville.—The cant., comprising 24 com., had a pop. of 13,235 in 1841.—The com. is 8 m. N of Abbeville, on the Maye river. Pop. 1,640.

CRE'CY-SUR-MORIN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, arrond. of Meaux. - The cant., comprising 22 com., had a pop. of 12,341 in 1841.—The com., 6 m. S of Meaux, on the r. bank of the Grand-Morin, has a pop. of 1,049. The town has an active trade in charcoal.

CRE'CY-SUR-SERRE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, arrond. of Laon.—The cant., comprising 22 com., had a pop. of 12,686 in 1841.—The com., 8 m. N of Laon, had a pop. of 2,034.
CRECORAGH, a parish in co. Limerick, 6 m. SW of Limerick. Area 3,013 acres. Pop. 1,284.
CREDENHILL, a parish in Hereford, 5 m. NW by W of Hereford. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. 192.
CRE'DIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Rohan. Pop. 1,734.

CREDIT, a river of Upper Canada, which rises in the mountains of Caledon, and, flowing through the townships of Chinguacousy, Esquesing, and Toronto, enters Lake Ontario at Port Credit. It is celebrated for its salmon-fishings. Immense quantities of lumber are sawn at the mills on this stream.

CREDITON, a parish and market-town of Devonshire.—Area of p. 11,440 acres. Pop. 5,947.—The town is situated on the Creedy, near its confluence with the Exe, 8 m. NW of Exeter. Serges, kerseymeres, sailcloth, and canvass, are extensively manufactured here, and there is a considerable trade in wool and yarns. The church is a fine building, and there is a richly endowed grammar school. C. is one of the polling-places for the members for N Devon. It was anciently the see of a bishop.

CREE, a river of Ayrshire, which rises on the skirts of Carrick, in two streams; the one issuing from Loch-Dornal, and known as the C. proper; the other rising on the S skirts of Eldrick hill, receiving an augmentation from Loch-Moan, and flowing S, under the name of the Minnock-water, till its junction with the C., where it expands into Loch-C., CRE about 13 m. below the Bridge-of-C. From the upper bridge of C., till it falls into Wigton bay by a considerable estuary, the C. divides Wigtonshire from Kirkcudbright. It is navigable for several miles up, and has been the chief source of all the agricultural improvements which have been made in this part of the country. It produces excellent fish of different kinds; salmon in considerable quantities. The smelt or sparling, a very rare fish, is also found in the C.
CREECH, a tything in the p. of Church-Knowle, Dorsetshire, 2 m. S of Wareham. Pop. 183.
CREECH-ST.-MICHAEL, a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. E by N of Taunton. Area 2,150 acres. Pop. 1,296.
CREED, a parish in Cornwall, 13 m. NNE of Tregony. Area 2,710 acres. Pop. 758.
CREED'S BRIDGE, a village in Princess Anne co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 136 m. SE by E of Richmond.
CREEK, a township in Washington co., in the state of Pennsylvania. Pop. 2,006.

CREEKS, or Muskhogees, an Indian nation, or confederacy of Indian tribes, which formerly inhabited a large part of the states of Georgia and Alabama, U. S., or the country bounded northward by about the 34th parallel, and extending from the Mobile river to the Atlantic ocean. The ridge that divided the Tombigbee or W branch of the Mobile from the Alabama, was the line that separated the Choctaws from the groups of tribes which were united in the Creek confederacy. Their territory, including all Florida, reached on the N to the Cherokees; on the NE and E to the country on the Savannah and to the Atlantic. Along the sea their N limit seems to have extended almost to Cape Fear. Their population, spread over a fourfold wider territory, did not exceed that of the Choctaws in number. Their towns were situated on the banks of the beautiful creeks in which the country abounded; the waters of their bold rivers, from the Coosa to the Chattahoochee, descended rapidly, with a clear current, through healthful and fertile regions; they were careful in their agriculture, and, before going to war, assisted their women to plant. In Florida, they welcomed the Spanish missionaries; and, throughout their country, derived so much benefit from the arts of civilization, that their numbers soon promised to increase; and, being placed between the English of Carolina, the French of Louisiana, and the Spaniards of Florida,—and bordering on the Choctaws, the Chickasas, and the Cherokees,—their political importance made them esteemed as the most powerful Indian nation N of the gulf of Mexico. They readily gave shelter to fugitives from other tribes; and their speech became so modified, that, with radical resemblances, it has the widest departure from its kindred dialects. The Yamassees, on the Savannah, seem to have been one of their bands; and the Seminoles of Florida are but "wild men" lost from their confederacy, and abandoning agriculture for the chase. Their present number is about 25,000; and they have all removed, under treaty with the United States government, to the W of the Mississippi, and are principally located on the Canadian river and its tributaries.
CREEKSEA, or CRIXETH, a parish in Essex, 2 m. NW of Burnham. Area 690 acres. Pop. 199.
CREEKSTOWN, a parish in co. Meath, 12 m. NNE of Ratoath. Pop. 195.

CREELSBURG, a village in Russell co., in Kentucky, 109 m. S of Frankfort. CREES,- the Salteurs or Knistenaux of the French Canadian traders,—a once powerful and numerous Indian nation, inhabiting the shores of Hudson's bay from Moose river to the mouth of Churchill river, and westward to the Athabasca lake and the plains which lie betwixt the forks of the Saskatchawan. All the nations which are within the limits of the United States N of the Ohio, and E of the Mississippi, speak languages which may be considered only as dialects of that spoken by the Crees and Chippeways. The C. are now, perhaps, the most harmless and inoffensive of the whole Indian race. Keevor gives the following description of the C. Indians: - "They are, for the most part, tall, large-boned, and long-visaged, with very prominent features; the eye is penetrating, and of a deep black colour; the nose prominent, of an aquiline shape, not at all flattened; the forehead short and straight; mouth large, the lips not in the least everted; the hair of a shining black, coarse, and uniformly straight. When viewed in profile, the parts appear more deeply and distinctly marked than in the Esquimeaux. The ear is not placed so far back on the head, nor is the space between the eyes so great as in the Esquimaux. The general expression of countenance is gloomy and severe. They have little hair on the chin or upper lip, owing to its being immediately eradicated on its first appearance. The females differ considerably in person and features from the men, being short, small-boned, with the face more rounded, with an expression of mildness and sweetness." They are good hunters; but have laid aside the bow and arrow, and use only guns. Tattooing is almost universal with them. A full and interesting account of the C., by Dr. Richardson, is given in the 3d chapter of Captain Franklin's Narrative of a Journey to the shores of the Polar sea in 1819-22. Lond. 4to. 1823.
CREESLOUGH, a village in the p. of Clondahorky, co. Donegal, 41 m. from Dunfanaghy. Pop. 235.
CREETING-ALL-SAINTS, a parish in Suffolk, 1 m. NE of Needham. Area 1,780 acres. Pop. 286.
CREETING-ST.-MARY, a parish in Suffolk, adjoining the above. Area 2,080 acres. Pop. 256.
CREETING-ST.-OLAVE, a parish in Suffolk, 13 m. N of Needham. Area returned with that of C .- St.-Mary. Pop. 30.
CREETING-ST.-PETER, a parish in Suffolk, 25 m. E by S of Stow-Market. Area returned with C.-All-Saints. Pop. 213.
CREETON, a parish in Lincolnshire, 31 m. SSE of Corby. Area 490 acres. Pop. 64.

CREETOWN, or FERRYTOWN OF CREE, a village in the p. of Kirkmabreck, stewartry of Kirkcudbright; 71 m. SE of Newton-Stewart, beautifully situated near the mouth of the Cree, on the E side of its estuary. It has a good anchorage, and is chiefly supported by its coasting-trade. Pop. 984.
CREEVE, a parish in cos. Roscommon and Connaught, 21 m. W of Carrick-on-Shannon. Area 4,574 acres, of which 745 acres are in Roscommon. Pop. 2,086.
CREFELT. See KREFELD.

CREGGAN, a parish in cos. Louth and Armagh, 71 m. NW of Dundalk. Area 24,815 acres. Pop. 15,502. The parish contains 68 townlands, which vary in size from 30 to 700 plantation acres. Though some subordinate districts are boggy, and some moorish, stony, and upland, the prevailing quality of the land is now good and now excellent. If the whole area be distributed into 20 parts, 4 or 41 will be found to be in actual tillage, 123 or 13 occasionally in tillage or naturally arable, and 3 boggy, moorish, under water, or otherwise waste. The surface is singularly bare of wood. Several slate quarries were long ago worked.
CREGGS, a village in the p. of Kilbegnet, co. Galway, 5½ m. N by E of Ballinamore. Pop. 163.
CREGLINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the principality of Ansbach, on the Tauber river. Pop. 1,400.
CREGRINA, a parish in Radnorshire, 6 m. E by N of Builth, on the Eddw. Pop. 112.
CREHELP, a parish in co. Wicklow, 8 m. SSW of Blessington. Area 2,215 acres. Pop. 665.
CREHEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of Plancoët, 8 m. NW of Dinan. Pop. 1,594.

CREIL, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Oise, arrond. of Senlis .- The cant., comprising 19 com., had a pop. of 15,708 in 1841.—The com. and town, 5 m. NW of Senlis, on the Oise river, had a pop. of 1,790 in 1841. Earthenware is largely manufactured here.

CREMA, a town of Austrian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, forming the E part of the deleg. of Lodi-et-Crema, 8 m. ENE of Lodi, on the r. bank of the Serio, at an alt. of 252 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 8,668. It is the see of a bishop, and has several handsome churches. It has manufactures of linen, lace, and silk. Excellent flax is grown in the environs, and occupies the trade and industry of the city.
CREMEAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Saint-Just-en-Chevalet. Pop. 1,350.

CREMIA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. and 16 m. NNE of Como, on the W shore of the lake of Como. Pop. 698. CRE dep. of Isère, arrond. of Tour-du-Pin.-The cant, comprising 29 com., had a pop. of 18,226 in 1841.- The town, 13 m. NW of Tour-du-Pin, had a pop. of 2,292.
CREMNITZ. See KREMNITZ.

CREMO'NA, a delegation or province of the Lombardo-Venetian kingdom; bounded on the N by the prov. of Brescia; on the E by that of Mantua; on the S by the Po, which divides it from the duchy of Parma; and on the W by the prov. of Lodi. Its length is about 40 m. from NW to SE; and its breadth 15 m. Area 526 sq. m. The Po, the Oglio, and the Adda are its principal rivers. Its surface forms part of the great level of Lombardy; and is nearly all under cultivation. Wine, oil, flax, and silk are extensively produced; and the rich pasture lands give a large return in the products of the dairy. In the lowest parts, along the Po, wine is the principal production. It is divided into 9 distretto or districts, which are subdivided into 186 communes. Pop. in 1837, 182,406. The districts are C., Soncino, Castel-Leone, Soresina, Pizzighettone, Robecco, Pieve-d'Olmi, Casalmaggiore, Piadena, and Pescarolo. There are in the prov. 141 elementary schools for boys, and 29 female schools. The two principal towns, besides the capital, are Casalmaggiore, and Pizzighettone, a strong fortress on the E bank of the Adda.

CREMONA, the capital of the above prov., and a bishop's see, is 45 m. SE of Milan, on the I. bank of the Po, at an alt. of 229 ft. above sea-level. It is surrounded by walls flanked with bastions, and by ditches, and is defended by a citadel. A navigable canal, which communicates with the Po and the Oglio, passes through the town; and the Po itself is navigable for large boats from C. to the sea. The town is 5 m. in circumf. The streets are spacious and regular, and there are several handsome edifices; yet, like most of the cities of Lombardy, it has a melancholy appearance from the evident signs of decay which here and there meet the observant eye. The cathedral is a Gothic building curiously ornamented with sculptures, and presenting a mixture of Saxon and Italian architecture. Many of its 44 churches are worthy of notice, also the town-house, and market. The tower or belfry of the cathedral, 372 ft. in height, and adorned with rich open work, is a conspicuous object for miles around from the plains of Lombardy.—C. is the residence of the governor of the prov.; and the seat of its civil, criminal, and commercial courts. It possesses a lyceum, a royal gymnasium, a public library of 15,000 vols., and a school of fine arts. It was the first city in Italy in which infant schools were established, in 1829, by the exertions of the Abate Aporti.—C. carries on a considerable trade in the agricultural produce of the surrounding district by means of the Po and the various canals communicating with that river. It has manufactures of silk and cotton; and long held a high reputation for its violins and musical strings, though its fame on this account is now only matter of history. - C. was originally in the territory of the Galli Cenomani. It was colonized by the Romans at the same time with Placentia, under T. Sempronius and P. Cornelius, and became a populous and flourishing town. In the war between Vitellius and Vespasian the citizens of C. sided with the former, upon which the victorious army of Vespasian pillaged and burnt the town, but it was rebuilt and had fresh privileges conferred upon it by Vespasian. After the fall of the empire it shared the vicissitudes of the other cities of Lombardy. After being destroyed a second time by the Lombards, it was rebuilt, in 1184, by the emperor Frederic I. Marshal Villeroy was surprised and taken

CRE'MIEU, a canton and town of France, in the | prisoner here by Prince Eugene in 1702. In 1796 ナンドゥグランド・マング・アルト・アルコン・アル・アル・アル・アル・アル・アル・アル・アル・アル・アル・アル・カル・カル・カル・カル・カル・カル・カル・カル・カル・カル・カル・カル・カル C. surrendered to the French; in 1799 it was retaken by the Austrians; in 1800 the French again got possession of it, and retained it till 1814.
CREMORGAN, or KILCOLMANBRACK, a parish in Queen's co., 3\frac{3}{4} m. S of Maryborough. Area 906 acres. Pop. 67.
CREMS. See KREMS.
CRENDON (Long), a parish in Bucks, 21 m. N of Thame. Area 3,120 acres. Pop. 1,656.
CRE'ON, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Gironde, arrond. of Bordeaux.- The cant., comprising 28 com., had a pop. of 14,790 in 1841.— The town, 9 m. SE of Bordeaux, had a pop. of 913.
CRE'PEY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Colombey. Pop. 1,003.
CRE'PIN (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, cant. of Guillestre, on the l. bank of the Durance, 9 m. NNE of Embrun. Pop. 1,210.
CREPIN-D'IRROUVILLERS (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Méru. Pop. 674.

CRE'PY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Oise, arrond. of Senlis.—The cant., comprising 24 com., had a pop. of 14,141 in 1841.— The town, 12 m. E of Senlis, had a pop. of 2,873. It has some trade in wood, wheat, coarse linens, and leather.
CRE'PY-EN-LAONNOIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. and 5 m. NW of Laon. Pop. 1,463.
CRE'QUY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Fruges. Pop. 1,401.

CRESCENT ISLAND, an island in the Pacific, in S lat. 23° 20', W. long. 134° 35'. It is 3\frac{1}{2} m. in length, and 11 m. in breadth, and consists of a strip of coral about 100 yards in width, having the sea on one side, and a lagune on the other. Its general height is only 2 ft. above the water; and the soil where highest is only 6 ft. above sea-level.

CRESCENTINO, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 18 m. WSW of Verceil, in a marshy district near the confluence of the Dora-Baltea and the Po. Pop. 4,000. It has two churches, several convents, and an hospital.
CRESCIANO, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, district of Riviera. Pop. 227, Catholics. Free-stone is quarried in the environs.
CRESPANO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the del. and 25 m. WNW of Treviso, district and 8 m. NW of Asolo. Pop. 2,023. It is noted for its manufactures of linen, and woollen fabrics.

CRESPIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Condé-sur-l'Escaut. Pop. 1,419. It has manufactories of sugar from beet-root.—Also a commune in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of La Salvetat. Pop. 1,122.

CRESPINO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice and del. of Polesina, 10 m. SE of Rovigo, on the l. bank of the Po. Pop. 4,000. It has an active trade in bricks, timber, lint, silk, and other articles of local produce.
CRESSA, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 15 m. NNW of Novara, near the l. bank of the Agogna. Pop. 1,051.
CRESSANGES, a commune of France, in the A fair is held once a-year. Freestone is quarried in dep. of the Allier, cant. of Montel. Pop. 1,196.
CRESSAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Creuse, cant. of Ahun, 13 m. ENE of Guéret. Pop. 1,691.
CRESSENSAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lot, cant. of Martel. Pop. 960. It is noted for its truffles.
CRESSIAT, a village of France, in the dep. of the Jura, cant. and 7 m. W of Orgilet. Pop. 1,000. CRE
CRESSIER, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 8 m. NE of Neufchatel, and 2 m. W of Landeron, near the l. bank of the Thiele. Pop. (Cath.) 850. The locality is noted for its wine.
CRESSIER - SUR - MORAT, or GRISSACH - OB -
MURTEN, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 5 m. NNW of Fribourg, on an affluent of Lake Morat. Pop. (Cath.) 314.
CRESSING, a parish of Essex, 3 m. SE of Braintree. Area 2,960 acres. Pop. 560.
CRESSINGHAM (GREAT), a parish of Norfolk, 41 m. WNW of Watton. Area 2,940 acres. Pop. 476.
CRESSINGHAM (LITTLE), a parish of Norfolk, 3 m. SW of Watton. Area 1,690 acres. Pop. 244.

CRESSWELL, a township in the p. of Woodhorn, Northumberland, 71 m. NE of Morpeth. Pop. 253. -Also a township in the p. of Elmton, Derbyshire. Pop. 222.—Also an extra-parochial district of Staffordshire, 3 m. SSW of Cheadle. Area 460 acres. Pop. 16.
CRESSY. See CRECY.

CREST, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Drome, arrond. of Die. The cant. comprises 28 com. Pop. in 1831, 22,877; in 1841, 24,104. The town is situated at the foot of a rocky hill, on the r. bank of the Drome, 19 m. E of Die, and 17 m. SSE of Valence, and is intersected by the road from Valence to Gap. Pop. in 1841, 4,948. It has a consistorial Calvinistic church, and a college, and on an adjacent eminence is the tower of C., a fine specimen of ancient architecture, and now used as a house-of-correction. It has extensive manufactories of woollen, silk, and cotton fabrics, and of sugar from beet-root, several dye-works, paper-mills, tanneries, &c., and an important entrepot trade in truffles and other articles of local produce.
CREST (LE), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Veyre, 7 m. SSE of Clermont-Ferrand. Pop. 1,268.
CRETACCIO, an island of the Adriatic, in the Tremiti group, to the N of the prov. of Capitanata.
CRETE. See CANDIA.
CRETE (LA), a commune and town of France, in dep. of the Haute-Marne, cant. and 3 m. SSE of Andelot, on the r. bank of the Rognon. Pop. 125. It has some iron and wire-works.

CRETEIL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. of Charenton-le-Pont, 7 m. SE of Paris, on a rising ground near the l. bank of the Marne. Pop. in 1846, 1,607. It has numerous spinning, fulling, and saw-mills, and extensive quarries of freestone and gypsum.
CRETINGHAM, a parish of Suffolk, 41 m. WSW of Framlington, on the Deben. Area 1,700 acres. Pop. 411.
CREUE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Meuse, cant. of Vigneulles. Pop. 742. It produces good wine.

CREULLY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados, arrond. of Caen. The cant. comprises 26 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,940; in 1841, 13,708. The town is on the r. bank of the Seule, 12 m. NW of Caen. Pop. 1,009. It has extensive manufactures of lace and tulle, and several tanneries. Grain forms its chief article of commerce. the environs.

CREUS (CAP DE), a promontory of Spain, on the Catalonian coast,—the Aphrodisium promontorium of the ancients,—in N lat. 42° 19' 35", E long. 3° 15' 48", forming the NE point of the peninsula to the N of the gulf of Rosas, and the most easterly point of Spain. It may be seen at 25 m. distance. The current here sets SW.

CREUSE, a central department of France, deriving its name from one of its principal rivers, and formed of portions of the ancient provinces of Poitou, Marche, Bourbonnais, Limousin, and Berry. It lies between the parallels of 45° 40', and 46° 28' N. It is bounded on the N by the deps. of Indre and Cher; on the NE by the dep. of Allier; on the E by that of Puy-de-Dôme; on the S by that of Corrèze; and on the W by Haute-Vienne. Its area is 556,399 hectares; and with the exception of a small portion of its S side, which belongs to the basin of the Garonne, it is wholly comprised within the basin of the Loire. It is an elevated and mountainous district, being intersected by several branches from the central mass of the Auvergnat mountains. The highest point is the Montagne-de-Sermur, alt. 2,427 ft. The general inclination of the surface is from S to N. The two principal chains of hills, running NNW and SSE, enclose between them the valley of the Creuse. This river rises in the SE part of the dep., at Masd'Artigues, near the frontiers of the dep. of Corrèze; and flows N past Felletin to Aubusson, receiving the waters of the Roseille on the r. a little above Aubusson; whence it flows NNW through nearly the whole length of the dep., and then forms the boundary between the deps. of Indre-et-Loire and La Vienne, and falls into the Vienne, a branch of the Loire. The Cher, a tributary of the Loire, rises in the E part of the dep.; also the Tardes, a tributary of the Cher. On the W side of the dep. are the Gartempe, an affluent of the Creuse; and the Ardour, an affluent of the Gartempe. On the SW side occur the Maude and the Thorion, both tributaries of the Vienne; and the Villeneuve, the Leyrenne, and the Visge, affluents of the Thorion.—The climate is generally cold, moist, and variable. The prevailing winds are from S to N. The soil of the dep. is far from fertile, except in a few of the valleys. About 54,240 hectares are heaths and waste lands; 27,120 h. are considered good arable soil; and 108,480 h. present a shallow stony soil. The want of good roads also greatly contributes to reduce the value of the land in this dep. far below the average of France. The produce in corn is comparatively small, and rye is the species of grain chiefly cultivated; and there is no wine made; but the pasturage is good, and a considerable quantity of horned cattle and sheepespecially the latter—are reared. In 1839, the livestock amounted to 6,178 horses, 160,779 head of cattle, 709,060 sheep, 58,039 pigs, and 8,013 goats.— The mineral productions of the dep. consist of coal, granite, potter's clay, gypsum, mica, and antimony. The quantity of coal raised in 1839 from 3 collieries was valued at 20,445 francs. Among the manufactures of this district are woollens, cottons, leather, paper, glass, porcelain, and the celebrated tapestries and carpets of Felletin and Aubusson. From 20,000 to 24,000 of the pop. annually migrate in small parties for 9 months to find work in the surrounding departments. Property is so much subdivided in this dep. that three-fourths of the estates are assessed at below 20 francs a-year. The dep. is divided into 4 arrondissements, viz.: Aubusson, Guéret, Boussac, and Bourganeuf, which are subdivided into 25 cantons and 266 communes.—The chief towns are Guéret, the capital, on the hills skirting the SW side of the valley of the C.; and Aubusson. The pop. of the dep. in 1801 was 218,041; in 1821, 248,783; in 1841, 278,029; and in 1846, 285,680. The dep. forms, with that of Haute-Vienne, the diocese of the bishop of Limoges. In 1842 this dep. contained 6 secondary establishments for education, with 437 pupils; 2 communal colleges, with 221 pupils; 1 normal school; and 324 elementary schools, with 13,504 papils in winter, and 8,946 in summer. Under the late regime it sent 4 deputies to the legislature. CRE
CREUTZ. See KREUTZ.
CREUTZWALD-LA-CROIX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Moselle, cant. of Bouzonville. Pop. 1,577. It has a glass and some ironworks, and contains several artesian wells.
CREUX-DE-VENT, or CREUX-DU-VAN, a summit of the Jura chain, on the confines of the cantons of Neufchatel and Vaud, 11 m. SW of Neufchatel, and 4,806 ft. above sea-level, in N lat. 46° 55′ 54".
CREUZIER-LE-VIEUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Allier, cant. of Cusset, 12 m. SW of La Palisse. Pop. 1,402. The environs afford good wine.

CREUZOT (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Montcenis, 15 m. SSE of Autun, at the extremity of the canal of the same name, which communicates with the Canal-du- Centre. Pop. in 1846, 3,050. It is noted for its crystal manufactory, and has extensive iron-works and coal-mines. The area of the coal basin of C. and Blancy is estimated at 30,631 hectares, and produced in 1839 2,195,285 quintals of coal, amounting in value to 1,839,159 francs.
CREVACUORE, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 25 m. NNW of Vercelli, on the l. bank of the Sessera, 4 m. W of its confluence with the Sesia. Pop. 920. Iron is found in the environs.
CREVALCUORE, a town of the Papal states, in the delegation and 19 m. NW of Bologna.

CREVANT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Indre, cant. of Aigurande, 9 m. S of La Châtre. Pop. 1,380.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Lezoux, near the Allier, 11 m. WNW of Thiers. Pop. 1,149. CREVECŒUR, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Oise, arrond. of Clermont. The cant. comprises 20 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,615; in 1841, 12,179. The town is 26 m. NW of Clermont. Pop. in 1846, 2,414. Woollen fabrics and hosiery are extensively manufactured here, and there are several carding mills. The trade consists chiefly in horses, grain, cider, and wool. Fairs are held three times a-year.—Also a fort of Holland, in the prov. of North Brabant, 4 m. N of Bois-le-Duc, at the confluence of the Diest with the Meuse.—Also a commune in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Mezidon. Pop. 324. It has manufactures of woollen fabrics, and is noted for its poultry.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Marcoing, 5 m. S of Cambrai, on the r. bank of the Escaut. Pop. in 1841, 2,022. It has manufactures of sugar from beet-root, and several lime-kilns.—Also a hamlet in the dep. of the Seine, cant. of St. Denis, and com. of La-Cour-Neuve. Pop. 195.

CREVECEUR, a Dutch fort and factory of Upper Guinea, on the Gold coast, in the state of Accra, 60 m. WSW of the mouth of the Volta, and a 1 m. E of Fort James. The fort is now in ruins. CREVECŒUR. See PEORIA.

CREVILLENTE, a town of Spain, in Valencia, in the prov. and 18 m. WSW of Alicante, and partido of Elche. Pop. 7,226. It has a parish-church, a school, an hospital, and a custom-house. Mats are extensively manufactured here.

CREVOLA, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 3 m. N of Ossola, near the r. bank of the Toce, and at the SE extremity of the Val-di-Dovedro. It has extensive glass-works.—The Ponte C., which crosses the Dovedro at its entrance into the Vald'Ossola, and by which the Simplon route is pursued to Duomo-d'Ossola, is one of the finest structures of the class in Europe.

CREWE, a town and township in the p. of Barthomley in Cheshire, 41 m. SSW of Sandbach, and 11 m. SW of Congleton. Area of township, 2,190 acres. Pop. in 1801, 289; in 1841, 396.—The town is a creation of the last six years, although it already numbers nearly 2,000 inhabitants. It occupies the acute angle formed by the junction of the Crewe and Chester railway with the Grand Junction railway; and has been erected by the proprietors of the latter railway for the accommodation of their workshops and engineering establishment, and the mechanics employed in them. The station is 1664 m. from London; 532 m. from Birmingham; 431 m. from Liverpool; and 214 m. from Chester. The following account of this interesting town is from the columns of the Liverpool Journal. The buildings exhibit all the resources of taste and skill in the formation of what, in strict accuracy, may be called a model-town. Elaborate decoration was not required; but art revealed itself in imparting beauty to dwellings of labourers; and an endless variety give a pleasing appearance to houses which less cultivated judgment would have rendered sombre, because uniform. The dwelling-houses arrange themselves in four classes: first, the villa-style lodges the superior officers; next, a kind of ornamented Gothic constitutes the houses of the next in authority; the engineers are domiciled in detached mansions, which accommodate four families, with gardens and separate entrances; and last, the labourer delights in neat cottages of four apartments, the entrances within ancient porches. The first, second, and third have all gardens and yards; the fourth has also gardens. The rooms are all capacious: the ground-floors are tiled, and, as the back and front are open, ventilation is perfect. Each house is supplied with gas and water. There are baths, play-ground, news-room, library, schools, and assembly-room. The houses are let to their occupants at rates from 2s. 9d. per week, upwards. C. has a town-council, popularly chosen. They sent 20 names to the directors, who selected 12, from whom they were to select 9: they did so, and the company added 3. These representatives manage all municipal matters, and regulate the library, newsroom, festivals, &c. They have no criminals. A church is built with Newcastle blue or variegated brick, in the style of early Norman, with tower, transept, &c., and is most comfortably fitted up inside for 700 persons. It occupies the centre of an open space, and on the l. are the boys' school, the girls' school, and the infants' school. The directors have endowed this church with £200 per ann. The workshops cover no less than 30 acres, in the more acute portion of the angle. On the r., you turn into a large apartment fitted up for building new waggons; it opens into another still larger, and here waggons are repaired. Further on is the forge where the iron work is executed. The fan is used instead of the bellows; but here, as in all the other smithies, bellows are erected in the event of the fan failing. Turning round from the waggon department, you enter the coach-building room, in continuation of which are the repairing shop and smithy attached. The next great wing of the building is devoted to the locomotive department. It presents the aspect of a vast polytechnic institution: all the vast implements of engineering science seem gathered together here. Planing machines of all forms and sizes fill up the centre, connected with endless straps to a powertransmitting drum; while on either side are lathes, punching, shearing, and cutting machines. Adjoining is the locomotive waiting-room, where engines are kept always ready under steam-pressure in case of accident, and where all undergo examination.
CREWKERNE, a parish of Somerset, 10 m. SSW of Ilchester, on the post-road, in a valley watered by branches of the Parrot and Axe. Area 5,810 acres. Pop. 4,414. CRI
CREYENBERG, a bailiwick of Saxe-Weimar, in the circle of Eisenach. Pop. 3,638. Its cap. is Tiefenort.

CREYSSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lot, cant. of Marcel, 20 m. NE of Gourdon. Pop. 1,364.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. and 4 m. from Bergerac. Pop. 874. It has several paper-mills, and produces good wine.
CRE'ZANCY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Cher, cant. of Sancerre. Pop. 1,449.

CRIALES, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 48 m. NNE of Burgos, partido of Villarcayo, near the r. bank of the Gerla. CRICARE', QUIRICARE, or SAO-MATHEOS, a river of Brazil, which takes its rise in the prov. of Minas- Geraes, to the SW of the Serra-das-Esmeraldas; directs its course ESE to Galveos, in the prov. of Bahia; thence bends NE; and discharges itself into the Atlantic in S lat. 18° 37'. See SAO-MATHEOS.

CRICCIETH, or CRICKEITH, a parish, borough, and market-town of Carnarvonshire, 9 m. NE of Pwllheli, and 4 m. SW of Tremadoc, pleasantly situated on a spacious bay. The village consists of only a few straggling cottages along the sea-shore. Pop. of p. 811; of borough 604.
CRICCIN, a township in the p. of Rhuddlan, Flintshire. Pop. 638.
CRICH, a parish of Derbyshire, 4 m. SW of Alfreton and 13 m. N of Derby, near the Derby and Leeds railway, and on the Cromford canal. Area 6,180 acres. Pop. 3,698.

CRICHTON, a parish and village in Edinburghshire, intersected by the Tyne.—The p. has an area of about 3,900 Scots acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,384, of whom 843 were in the v. of Path-head, and 122 in the v. of C. Lime is extensively manufactured in this p. The remains of the ancient castle of C. are highly interesting.

CRICK, a hamlet in the p. of Caerwent, Monmouthshire, 4 m. WSW of Chepstow, on the postroad. Pop. 148.—Also a parish and v. of Northamptonshire, 7 m. N of Daventry, near the London and Birmingham railway, which has a station here 7 m. from Rugby; and bisected by the Derby and Leicester canal. Area 3,930 acres. Pop. 1,006.
CRICKADARN (North and South), a parish of Breconshire, 7 m. NNW of Talgarth. Pop. 441.
CRICKET-MALHERBIE, a parish of Somerset, 24 m. SE of Ilminster, near the Charch canal. Area 350 acres. Pop. 36.
CRICKET-ST.-THOMAS, a parish of Somerset, 44 m. SW of Crewkerne. Area 540 acres. Pop. 78.
CRICKHEATH, a township in the p. of Oswestry, Salop. Pop. 370.

CRICKHOWELL, a parish and market-town of Breconshire, 14 m. ESE of Brecon, on the Brecon and Newport canal. The town, which is well-built, occupies a romantic situation, on a gentle acclivity, on the E bank of the Uske. Pop. 1,257.

CRICKLADE, a borough and market-town, Wilts, 7 m. NNE of Wootton-Basset, 44 m. NNW of Salisbury, on the N bank of the Thames, at the junction of the Churn and Key, and intersected by the Thames and Severn canal. Area 5,840 acres. Pop. 34,381. —The town of C. had a pop. of 2,128 in 1841. It is of high antiquity, and a borough by prescription: the principal functionary being the bailiff. From the time of Henry VI. till 1782, this borough regularly returned two members to parliament. Having been then, however, convicted of corrupt practices, the elective franchise was extended to the freeholders of the four adjoining hundreds, viz., Highworth, Staple. Kingsbridge, and Malmesbury, excepting such parts of the hundred of Malmesbury as are now included in the borough of Malmesbury. The polling-places are C., Brinckworth, and Swindon. The registered electors for 1837 were 1,636; in 1847, 1,656.
CRICKLAS, a hamlet in the p. of Abergwilly, Carmarthenshire. Pop. 474.
CRICQ (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Landes, cant. of Hagetman. Pop. 1,142.
CRICRIM, or CRYCRIM, a parish in counties Carlow and Wicklow. Area 2,471 acres. Pop. 732.
CRIDLING-STUBBS, a township in the p. of Womersley, W. R. of Yorkshire, 4½ m. E of Pontefract, on the Egborough and Goole canal. Area 1,380 aeres. Pop. 159.

CRIECH, or CREICH, a parish in the NE of Fifeshire. Area 2,314 acres. Pop. 430.--Also a parish in the S of Sutherlandshire, 40 m. in extent from E to W, and varying from 2 to 10 m. in breadth. Pop. 2,582. About a thirtieth part only of the surface is under cultivation.

CRIEFF, a small central parish of Perthshire, in the district of Strathearn, divided by the intersection of the p. of Monzie, into two districts of naturally different features, highland and lowland. The highland division consists chiefly of the strath of Glenalmond, with a pop. of 230. The soil of the lowland division, extending to 3,800 acres, is chiefly light and gravelly; in the vicinity of the town of C. it is loam. Pop. of the town and parish in 1801, 2,876; in 1831, 4,786; in 1841, 4,333.—The town of C. is 56 m. NW of Edinburgh, and 17 m. W of Perth. It is built on a gently rising ground, about a m. N of the Earn, and near the foot of the Grampians. It is the second town in Perthshire, and is much resorted to in the summer-months by invalids. The leading manufacture is a kind of thin linen called Silesias, and the weaving of cotton goods for the Glasgow manufacturers. There are about 500 weavers' looms in the parish; and a woollen manufactory has recently been established. There are also several distilleries and tanneries, and extensive flour-mills. Until the establishment of the Falkirk trysts in 1770, C. was the great Scottish market for the sale of black cattle.
CRIEL, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Eu, on the r. bank of the Yeres, 13 m. NE of Dieppe. Pop. 1,295. It has an hospital.
